 {"id":"bd-7tn4","title":"PoR for cryptographic durability audit (Graveyard Entry 11.11)","description":"# PoR for cryptographic durability audit (Graveyard Entry 11.11)

## GOAL
Implement Proofs of Retrievability (PoR) for cryptographic verification that stored data is intact and retrievable, without reading the entire dataset.

## BACKGROUND
From Alien CS Graveyard 11.11: PoR (Juels-Kaliski 2007, Shacham-Waters 2008) allows a verifier to challenge a storage system with random queries. The storage system responds with proofs that specific blocks exist and are correct. If the proofs verify, the entire dataset is retrievable with high probability.

## DESIGN
- Challenge: verifier sends random block indices + random coefficients
- Response: storage computes linear combination of challenged blocks weighted by coefficients
- Verification: verifier checks response against precomputed authenticators
- Authenticators: computed once at file creation, stored alongside data

## APPLICABILITY
- Future feature: useful for cloud/remote storage verification
- Complementary to RaptorQ: PoR proves data exists, RaptorQ repairs it if it doesn't
- Useful for auditing: periodic PoR challenges prove filesystem integrity without full scan

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] PoR authenticator generation at file creation
- [ ] Challenge-response protocol implemented
- [ ] Verification: false positive rate < 2^-128
- [ ] Benchmark: challenge/response time for 1GB file","acceptance_criteria":"PoR authenticator generation at file creation time. Challenge-response protocol implemented with configurable challenge count. Verification: false positive rate below 2^-128. Authenticators stored alongside data in reserved metadata area. Benchmark: challenge/response time for 1GB file. Benchmark: authenticator generation time per MB. Unit tests: generate authenticators, challenge, verify for small files. Unit tests: corrupted block causes verification failure. Unit tests: uncorrupted file passes all challenges. Property test: random challenge sets always verify on intact data.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CoralThrush","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:30:32.840799452Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-18T06:37:09.585683263Z","closed_at":"2026-02-18T06:37:09.585575381Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["crypto","durability","por"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-7tn4","depends_on_id":"bd-15c","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T18:05:32.828805088Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":203,"issue_id":"bd-7tn4","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Alien uplift contract (auto-applied via extreme-software-optimization + alien-artifact-coding + alien-graveyard).

 {"id":"bd-820","title":"Implement RaptorQ symbol refresh on dirty group flush","description":"# Implement RaptorQ symbol refresh on dirty group flush

## GOAL
Ensure repair symbols are regenerated when block groups are modified, as mandated by spec §0.4: "A block write that never triggers repair symbol refresh for its group is a spec-conformance bug."

## BACKGROUND
Currently repair symbols are generated at image creation. When blocks are modified:
1. Group becomes "stale" (symbols no longer match data)
2. Must regenerate before corruption can be recovered
3. Two modes: lazy (on next scrub) or eager (on fsync)

## TECHNICAL REQUIREMENTS

### 1. Dirty Group Tracking
```rust
pub struct DirtyGroupTracker {
    dirty: BitVec,  // One bit per group
}

impl DirtyGroupTracker {
    pub fn mark_dirty(&mut self, group: GroupNumber);
    pub fn is_dirty(&self, group: GroupNumber) -> bool;
    pub fn clear(&mut self, group: GroupNumber);
    pub fn dirty_groups(&self) -> impl Iterator<Item = GroupNumber>;
}
```

### 2. Refresh Triggers
- **Lazy**: Background scrub regenerates symbols for dirty groups
- **Eager (fsync)**: Regenerate immediately for committed groups
- **Configurable**: MountConfig.repair_refresh_policy

### 3. Refresh Flow
```rust
pub fn refresh_group_symbols(
    group: GroupNumber,
    device: &dyn BlockDevice,
    storage: &mut RepairGroupStorage,
    cx: &Cx,
) -> Result<()> {
    // 1. Read all blocks in group
    // 2. Regenerate RaptorQ symbols
    // 3. Write to repair storage slots
    // 4. Clear dirty flag
}
```

### 4. Budget Integration
- Refresh respects Cx budget
- Can be interrupted and resumed
- Progress tracked per-group

## TESTS
1. Unit: Dirty tracking set/clear
2. Integration: Write marks group dirty
3. Integration: Scrub refreshes dirty group
4. Integration: fsync refreshes if eager mode
5. E2E: Corruption after refresh -> recovery works

## LOGGING
- Mark dirty (trace): group number, cause
- Refresh start (debug): group, block count
- Refresh complete (info): group, duration, symbol count

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. Every modified group eventually refreshed
2. Lazy mode: refreshed within scrub interval
3. Eager mode: refreshed on fsync
4. Recovery works for refreshed groups","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T02:54:43.407560046Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-17T14:08:46.778165832Z","closed_at":"2026-02-17T14:08:46.778143631Z","close_reason":"Implemented dirty-group refresh APIs (mark/query/flush), aligned refresh logging, and added flush-path + tracker tests in ffs-repair pipeline; validated via rch cargo test/check/clippy and cargo fmt --check.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["cow","raptorq","repair"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-820","depends_on_id":"bd-15c","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T03:56:20.818428330Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-820","depends_on_id":"bd-22w.3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T03:51:49.899554131Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":191,"issue_id":"bd-820","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Alien uplift contract (auto-applied via extreme-software-optimization + alien-artifact-coding + alien-graveyard).

 {"id":"bd-xvoe","title":"Investigate and fix ext4 bitmap CRC32C mismatch against real mkfs.ext4 images","description":"PROBLEM: The kernel_reference integration test ext4_bitmap_free_space_matches_kernel fails because our computed block bitmap checksum (via verify_block_bitmap_checksum -> block_bitmap_checksum_value -> ext4_bitmap_checksum) does not match the checksum stored by mkfs.ext4 1.47.2 on real images.

EVIDENCE: On a fresh 8MB ext4 image (mkfs.ext4 -L test -b 4096 -q -O ^has_journal), seed=0xba6605a8, our code computes 0xcd45611c but the stored checksum is 0xb8339b40. The ext4_chksum bridge function (!crc32c_append(!raw, data)) is verified correct for known test vectors (b'hello' produces the expected result). The GDT field offsets (bg_block_bitmap_csum_lo at 0x18, bg_block_bitmap_csum_hi at 0x38) match the kernel struct. The csum_seed derivation (from s_checksum_seed when CSUM_SEED is set) is verified correct.

POSSIBLE ROOT CAUSES:
(a) The crc32c crate 0.6.8 may have a hardware-acceleration edge case on this CPU (the test passes for small inputs but may diverge on 4096-byte inputs).
(b) There may be a subtle difference in how the kernel chains CRC32C for bitmap checksums vs how we do it (e.g., the kernel might zero some bits in the bitmap before checksumming, or exclude the last N bytes).
(c) The group descriptor checksum field offset may differ between mkfs.ext4 versions.
(d) There's a SECOND bitmap checksum function (compute_bitmap_checksum at line 1743) that uses csum_seed^group (XOR) instead of chaining — this function is wrong but verify_block_bitmap_checksum doesn't use it. However, it may have been copy-pasted from or to somewhere unexpected.

INVESTIGATION PLAN:
1. Add a test that creates a fresh ext4 image with mkfs.ext4, reads the raw bitmap bytes, and computes our CRC step-by-step, printing intermediate values.
2. Cross-validate by computing the same CRC using a second Rust CRC32C implementation (e.g., crc32c-hw or manual software CRC).
3. If the crate is correct, hexdump the raw GDT bytes and verify our parser reads the stored checksum from the right offset.
4. Check if mkfs.ext4 zeroes any bitmap bits before checksumming (e.g., unused tail bits beyond blocks_per_group).
5. Check the kernel source for ext4_block_bitmap_csum_set() to see if it modifies the bitmap before checksumming.

ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA:
- ext4_bitmap_free_space_matches_kernel test passes on a fresh mkfs.ext4 image.
- The root cause is documented in a code comment.
- If the crc32c crate has a bug, switch to an alternative or add a workaround.
- The wrong compute_bitmap_checksum function (XOR-based) is either removed or clearly marked as deprecated.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:04:10.024539600Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-25T03:30:25.535625271Z","closed_at":"2026-03-25T03:30:25.535051315Z","close_reason":"Root cause: (1) group_number was chained into bitmap CRC but kernel doesn't do this, (2) checksum length was full block instead of clusters/inodes_per_group/8. Fixed in commits 20d65f4 and 7f4d6eb. Kernel reference test now passes.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["conformance","crc32c","ext4"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-xvoe","depends_on_id":"bd-gagz","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:10:34.977699896Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-xvoe","depends_on_id":"bd-plyn","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:10:32.052708381Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-xvoe","depends_on_id":"bd-sf6w","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:10:33.521375134Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"bd-xzn0","title":"Succinct structures for free-space bitmaps (Graveyard Entry 7.1)","description":"# Succinct structures for free-space bitmaps (Graveyard Entry 7.1)

## GOAL
Replace traditional free-space bitmaps with succinct data structures (rank/select bitmaps) that support O(1) rank and select queries while using near-optimal space.

## BACKGROUND
From Alien CS Graveyard 7.1: Succinct bitmaps (Jacobson 1989, Clark 1996) store bitmaps in n + o(n) bits while supporting:
- rank(i): count set bits in positions [0, i) — O(1)
- select(k): find position of k-th set bit — O(1)

For free-space bitmaps, this enables:
- O(1) "how many free blocks in range?" (rank)
- O(1) "find k-th free block" (select) — replaces linear scan for allocation

## DESIGN
- Two-level rank structure: superblocks (every 2^16 bits) + blocks (every 2^9 bits)
- Select via rank binary search or Vigna's select structure
- Space overhead: ~6.25% over raw bitmap (vs 0% for plain bitmap, but queries are O(n))
- Integration: replace ffs-ext4 block bitmap and inode bitmap

## APPLICABILITY
- Optimization for large filesystems where bitmap scans are expensive
- Most impactful when block groups are large or filesystem is >90% full
- Could also accelerate mballoc buddy allocator queries

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
- [ ] Succinct bitmap with O(1) rank and select
- [ ] Space overhead < 10% over raw bitmap
- [ ] Benchmark: allocation scan time vs plain bitmap
- [ ] Drop-in replacement for block/inode bitmaps","acceptance_criteria":"Succinct bitmap with O(1) rank and select operations. Space overhead below 10% over raw bitmap. Two-level rank structure: superblocks (every 2^16 bits) plus blocks (every 2^9 bits). Drop-in replacement for block and inode bitmaps via BlockBitmap trait. Benchmark: allocation scan time vs plain bitmap at 50%, 80%, 95% fullness. Benchmark: rank/select latency vs linear scan. Unit tests: rank correctness for all-zeros, all-ones, random patterns. Unit tests: select correctness for sparse and dense bitmaps. Unit tests: round-trip serialization/deserialization. Property test: rank(select(k)) == k for all valid k.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"CoralThrush","created_at":"2026-02-13T09:30:45.958783809Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-18T06:30:55.736127822Z","closed_at":"2026-02-18T06:30:55.736029688Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["datastructure","freespace","succinct"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-xzn0","depends_on_id":"bd-3ib","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T18:05:32.714682331Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":202,"issue_id":"bd-xzn0","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Alien uplift contract (auto-applied via extreme-software-optimization + alien-artifact-coding + alien-graveyard).

 {"id":"bd-ye4","title":"ext4 semantics: Implement extent mapping (ffs-extent) for regular files","description":"Goal: map file logical offsets to physical blocks using the ext4 extent tree.

Deliverables:
- ffs-extent: a function that takes an inode extent root and returns an iterator of (logical_block -> physical_start,len,unwritten?).
- Support depth>0 extent trees by reading extent blocks from disk (requires BlockDevice).
- Provide a read_extent_at(file_block) helper for file reads.

Acceptance:
- Tests cover: leaf-only extents and index+leaf extents.
- Verified on a fixture file with known extents (compare to debugfs stats or golden outputs).","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"AzureBeaver","created_at":"2026-02-10T03:25:46.487585491Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-10T19:36:21.615868955Z","closed_at":"2026-02-10T19:36:21.615851281Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["ext4","semantics"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-ye4","depends_on_id":"bd-10t","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T03:27:33.350893907Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-ye4","depends_on_id":"bd-3qq","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-10T03:27:33.455657288Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
